Name: training-free_spectral_routing_mixed_precision
Title: Spectral Routing for Training-Free Mixed-Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: Spectral decomposition of weight matrices can serve as a training-free proxy for determining optimal mixed-precision configurations, leveraging the intrinsic structure of the model to allocate bit-widths efficiently.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods often rely on heuristic proxies or computationally expensive search algorithms. Training-free approaches like EMQ and SliM-LLM have improved efficiency but still lack a mechanistic understanding of how quantization affects model performance. Recent work on spectral decomposition (e.g., Intrinsic Structure as a Proxy for Saliency) suggests that the principal components of weight matrices are critical for model accuracy. Our proposal builds on these insights by using spectral diagnostics to guide bit-width allocation, offering a mathematically transparent and deterministic approach to mixed-precision quantization.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) is a powerful technique for compressing deep neural networks, but determining the optimal bit-width configuration remains a challenge. Current methods often rely on heuristic proxies or computationally expensive search algorithms, limiting their scalability and interpretability. We propose a novel training-free approach that leverages spectral decomposition of weight matrices to guide bit-width allocation. By identifying the principal components of each layer, we can allocate higher precision to the most critical weights while aggressively quantizing less important ones. This approach provides a mathematically transparent and deterministic framework for MPQ, ensuring numerical stability and efficient resource utilization. We validate our method on a range of vision and language models, demonstrating significant improvements in accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Spectral Analysis and Bit-width Allocation. Perform singular value decomposition (SVD) on the weight matrices of each layer. Allocate higher precision (e.g., 8-bit) to the top-k singular values and lower precision (e.g., 4-bit) to the remaining weights. Evaluate the impact on model accuracy and inference speed.

Experiment 2: Comparison with Heuristic Proxies. Compare our spectral routing approach with existing heuristic proxies (e.g., Hessian-based methods) on a range of models, including ResNet, MobileNet, and LLaMA. Metrics: accuracy, memory usage, and inference time.

Experiment 3: Robustness to Noise. Introduce controlled noise to the weight matrices and observe the impact of spectral routing on model performance. Metrics: robustness score and accuracy degradation.

Experiment 4: Scalability to Large Models. Apply spectral routing to large language models (LLMs) and evaluate its effectiveness in reducing memory usage while maintaining accuracy. Metrics: perplexity and memory footprint.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of spectral routing may vary across different architectures and tasks, potentially limiting its generalizability.
SVD computation can be computationally expensive for very large models, although we mitigate this by performing decomposition only once per layer.
The method assumes that the principal components are the most critical for model performance, which may not hold for all layers or tasks.
